question:
4. For a fixed positive integer \( n \) let \( x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n} \) be real numbers satisfying \( 0 \leq x_{k} \leq 1 \) for \( k=1,2, \ldots, n \). Determine the maximum value, as a function of \( n \), of the sum of the \( n(n-1) / 2 \) terms:
\[
\sum_{\substack{i . j=1 \\ i<j}}^{n}\left|x_{i}-x_{j}\right|
\]
solution:
Solution. If we keep all but one of the \( x \) 's, say \( x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots, x_{n} \), fixed, then the sum in question is a convex function of \( x_{1} \), since it is a sum of convex functions. Hence the maximum value is achieved either for \( x_{1}=0 \) or for \( x_{1}=1 \); conceivably it could be achieved for both and even for all intermediate values.

Since the set of \( n \)-tuples being considered is a bounded closed set in \( \mathbf{R}_{n} \), a maximum is achieved at some point. We can then change any one of the \( x \) 's not already either a 0 or a 1 into either a 0 or a 1 without decreasing the sum. Hence among the points where the maximum is achieved there is at least one for which every \( x \) is either a 0 or a 1 . Consider this point.

If \( p \) of the \( x \) 's are zero and \( (n-p) \) are one, then the sum is clearly
\[
p(n-p)=\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{n}{2}-p\right)^{2}
\]
and this will be largest when \( p=n / 2 \) if \( n \) is even, or \( p=(n \pm 1) / 2 \) if \( n \) is odd.

The maximum value of the sum is therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{4} n^{2} \quad \text { if } n \text { is even, } \\
\frac{1}{4}\left(n^{2}-1\right) \text { if } n \text { is odd. }
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If \( n \) is even, then the maximum is achieved only if half the \( x \) 's are at one end and half at the other. But if \( n \) is odd, \( (n-1) / 2 x \) 's must be at each end, while the last \( x \) may be taken anywhere on the interval.

descriptive_long.question:
4. For a fixed positive integer \( totalnum \) let \( entryone, entrytwo, \ldots, entryn \) be real numbers satisfying \( 0 \leq entryk \leq 1 \) for \( k=1,2, \ldots, totalnum \). Determine the maximum value, as a function of \( totalnum \), of the sum of the \( totalnum(totalnum-1) / 2 \) terms:
\[
\sum_{\substack{indexone . indextwo=1 \\ indexone<indextwo}}^{totalnum}\left|entryi-entryj\right|
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. If we keep all but one of the \( realseq \)'s, say \( entrytwo, entrytri, \ldots, entryn \), fixed, then the sum in question is a convex function of \( entryone \), since it is a sum of convex functions. Hence the maximum value is achieved either for \( entryone=0 \) or for \( entryone=1 \); conceivably it could be achieved for both and even for all intermediate values.

Since the set of \( totalnum \)-tuples being considered is a bounded closed set in \( \mathbf{euclidspc} \), a maximum is achieved at some point. We can then change any one of the \( realseq \)'s not already either a 0 or a 1 into either a 0 or a 1 without decreasing the sum. Hence among the points where the maximum is achieved there is at least one for which every \( realseq \) is either a 0 or a 1. Consider this point.

If \( zerotally \) of the \( realseq \)'s are zero and \( (totalnum-zerotally) \) are one, then the sum is clearly
\[
zerotally(totalnum-zerotally)=\left(\frac{totalnum}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{totalnum}{2}-zerotally\right)^{2}
\]
and this will be largest when \( zerotally=totalnum / 2 \) if \( totalnum \) is even, or \( zerotally=(totalnum \pm 1) / 2 \) if \( totalnum \) is odd.

The maximum value of the sum is therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{4} totalnum^{2} \quad \text { if } totalnum \text { is even, } \\
\frac{1}{4}\left(totalnum^{2}-1\right) \text { if } totalnum \text { is odd. }
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If \( totalnum \) is even, then the maximum is achieved only if half the \( realseq \)'s are at one end and half at the other. But if \( totalnum \) is odd, \( (totalnum-1) / 2 \, realseq \)'s must be at each end, while the last \( realseq \) may be taken anywhere on the interval.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. For a fixed positive integer \( harmonica \) let \( snowflake, dandelion, \ldots, stingray \) be real numbers satisfying \( 0 \leq buttercup \leq 1 \) for \( k=1,2, \ldots, harmonica \). Determine the maximum value, as a function of \( harmonica \), of the sum of the \( harmonica(harmonica-1) / 2 \) terms:
\[
\sum_{\substack{woodpecker . alligator=1 \\ woodpecker<alligator}}^{harmonica}\left|moonstone-blackbird\right|
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. If we keep all but one of the \( meadowlark \)'s, say \( dandelion, rainforest, \ldots, stingray \), fixed, then the sum in question is a convex function of \( snowflake \), since it is a sum of convex functions. Hence the maximum value is achieved either for \( snowflake=0 \) or for \( snowflake=1 \); conceivably it could be achieved for both and even for all intermediate values.

Since the set of \( harmonica \)-tuples being considered is a bounded closed set in \( \mathbf{wanderlust} \), a maximum is achieved at some point. We can then change any one of the \( meadowlark \)'s not already either a 0 or a 1 into either a 0 or a 1 without decreasing the sum. Hence among the points where the maximum is achieved there is at least one for which every \( meadowlark \) is either a 0 or a 1. Consider this point.

If \( pineapple \) of the \( meadowlark \)'s are zero and \( (harmonica-pineapple) \) are one, then the sum is clearly
\[
pineapple(harmonica-pineapple)=\left(\frac{harmonica}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{harmonica}{2}-pineapple\right)^{2}
\]
and this will be largest when \( pineapple=harmonica / 2 \) if \( harmonica \) is even, or \( pineapple=(harmonica \pm 1) / 2 \) if \( harmonica \) is odd.

The maximum value of the sum is therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{4} harmonica^{2} \quad \text { if } harmonica \text { is even, } \\
\frac{1}{4}\left(harmonica^{2}-1\right) \text { if } harmonica \text { is odd. }
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If \( harmonica \) is even, then the maximum is achieved only if half the \( meadowlark \)'s are at one end and half at the other. But if \( harmonica \) is odd, \( (harmonica-1) / 2 meadowlark \)'s must be at each end, while the last \( meadowlark \) may be taken anywhere on the interval.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
4. For a fixed positive integer \( nullitymeasure \) let \( fixedconstantone, fixedconstanttwo, \ldots, fixedconstantn \) be real numbers satisfying \( 0 \leq fixedconstantk \leq 1 \) for \( k=1,2, \ldots, nullitymeasure \). Determine the maximum value, as a function of \( nullitymeasure \), of the sum of the \( nullitymeasure(nullitymeasure-1) / 2 \) terms:
\[
\sum_{\substack{i . terminalindex=1 \\ i<terminalindex}}^{nullitymeasure}\left|fixedconstanti-fixedconstantj\right|
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. If we keep all but one of the \( fixedconstant \)'s, say \( fixedconstanttwo, fixedconstantthree, \ldots, fixedconstantn \), fixed, then the sum in question is a convex function of \( fixedconstantone \), since it is a sum of convex functions. Hence the maximum value is achieved either for \( fixedconstantone=0 \) or for \( fixedconstantone=1 \); conceivably it could be achieved for both and even for all intermediate values.

Since the set of \( nullitymeasure \)-tuples being considered is a bounded closed set in \( \mathbf{complexspace} \), a maximum is achieved at some point. We can then change any one of the \( fixedconstant \)'s not already either a 0 or a 1 into either a 0 or a 1 without decreasing the sum. Hence among the points where the maximum is achieved there is at least one for which every \( fixedconstant \) is either a 0 or a 1. Consider this point.

If \( onesabundance \) of the \( fixedconstant \)'s are zero and \( (nullitymeasure-onesabundance) \) are one, then the sum is clearly
\[
onesabundance(nullitymeasure-onesabundance)=\left(\frac{nullitymeasure}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{nullitymeasure}{2}-onesabundance\right)^{2}
\]
and this will be largest when \( onesabundance=nullitymeasure / 2 \) if \( nullitymeasure \) is even, or \( onesabundance=(nullitymeasure \pm 1) / 2 \) if \( nullitymeasure \) is odd.

The maximum value of the sum is therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{4} nullitymeasure^{2} \quad \text { if } nullitymeasure \text { is even, } \\
\frac{1}{4}\left(nullitymeasure^{2}-1\right) \text { if } nullitymeasure \text { is odd. }
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If \( nullitymeasure \) is even, then the maximum is achieved only if half the \( fixedconstant \)'s are at one end and half at the other. But if \( nullitymeasure \) is odd, \( (nullitymeasure-1) / 2 fixedconstant \)'s must be at each end, while the last \( fixedconstant \) may be taken anywhere on the interval.

garbled_string.question:
4. For a fixed positive integer \( qazwsxed \) let \( zlmqwert, kdjfhgso, \ldots, poiulkjh \) be real numbers satisfying \( 0 \leq hjgrksla \leq 1 \) for \( k=1,2, \ldots, qazwsxed \). Determine the maximum value, as a function of \( qazwsxed \), of the sum of the \( qazwsxed(qazwsxed-1) / 2 \) terms:
\[
\sum_{\substack{vhgtrews . qwplmokn=1 \\ vhgtrews<qwplmokn}}^{qazwsxed}\left|mnbvcxza-plokmijn\right|
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. If we keep all but one of the \( qzxwvtnp \)'s, say \( kdjfhgso, asdfghjk, \ldots, poiulkjh \), fixed, then the sum in question is a convex function of \( zlmqwert \), since it is a sum of convex functions. Hence the maximum value is achieved either for \( zlmqwert=0 \) or for \( zlmqwert=1 \); conceivably it could be achieved for both and even for all intermediate values.

Since the set of \( qazwsxed \)-tuples being considered is a bounded closed set in \( \mathbf{edcrfvtg} \), a maximum is achieved at some point. We can then change any one of the \( qzxwvtnp \)'s not already either a 0 or a 1 into either a 0 or a 1 without decreasing the sum. Hence among the points where the maximum is achieved there is at least one for which every \( qzxwvtnp \) is either a 0 or a 1. Consider this point.

If \( rtyuiofg \) of the \( qzxwvtnp \)'s are zero and \( (qazwsxed-rtyuiofg) \) are one, then the sum is clearly
\[
rtyuiofg(qazwsxed-rtyuiofg)=\left(\frac{qazwsxed}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{qazwsxed}{2}-rtyuiofg\right)^{2}
\]
and this will be largest when \( rtyuiofg=qazwsxed / 2 \) if \( qazwsxed \) is even, or \( rtyuiofg=(qazwsxed \pm 1) / 2 \) if \( qazwsxed \) is odd.

The maximum value of the sum is therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{4} qazwsxed^{2} \quad \text { if } qazwsxed \text { is even, } \\
\frac{1}{4}\left(qazwsxed^{2}-1\right) \text { if } qazwsxed \text { is odd. }
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If \( qazwsxed \) is even, then the maximum is achieved only if half the \( qzxwvtnp \)'s are at one end and half at the other. But if \( qazwsxed \) is odd, \( (qazwsxed-1) / 2 qzxwvtnp \)'s must be at each end, while the last \( qzxwvtnp \) may be taken anywhere on the interval.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and d \geq  1 be fixed integers.  For k = 1,\ldots ,n let  
x_k = ((x_k)_1,(x_k)_2,\ldots ,(x_k)^d) \in  \mathbb{R}^d satisfy  

(i)   -2 \leq  (x_k)_j \leq  4  for every 1 \leq  j \leq  d,  

(ii)  x_1 + x_2 + \ldots  + x_n = 0  (the n vectors have the origin as their centroid).  

Determine, in closed form, the maximum possible value of  

  S(n,d) =  \Sigma _{1 \leq  i<j \leq  n}  \|x_i - x_j\|_2^2.

Give your answer as an explicit function of n and d.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  A convenient re-write of the target expression  
For any collection of vectors we have the identity  

  \Sigma _{i<j} \|x_i - x_j\|_2^2 = n \Sigma _{k=1}^n \|x_k\|_2^2 - \|\Sigma _{k=1}^n x_k\|_2^2.    (\star )

Assumption (ii) gives \Sigma  x_k = 0, so (\star ) reduces to  

  S(n,d) = n \Sigma _{k=1}^n \|x_k\|_2^2.                                (1)

Hence maximising S is equivalent to maximising  
  T := \Sigma _{k=1}^n \|x_k\|_2^2                                      (2)
under the same constraints (i)-(ii).

Step 2.  Separating the coordinates  
Because the squared norm is additive over the coordinates,  

  T = \Sigma _{j=1}^d \Sigma _{k=1}^n (x_k)_j^2 = \Sigma _{j=1}^d  T_j,  

where for each fixed coordinate j  

  T_j := \Sigma _{k=1}^n y_k^2 with y_k = (x_k)_j,  -2 \leq  y_k \leq  4, \Sigma _{k=1}^n y_k = 0.   (3)

The d coordinates are independent: the global maximum of T is the sum of the
individual maxima of the d one-dimensional problems (3).  
Thus it suffices to solve (3) once; the answer will then be multiplied by d.

Step 3.  One dimension - extremal structure  
Fix a coordinate and write y_1,\ldots ,y_n for that coordinate.  The set  
F(y_1,\ldots ,y_n)=\Sigma  y_k^2 is a strictly convex function of every variable.
Hence, by the usual ``pushing to the boundary'' argument, in every maximiser
all but at most one of the y_k's must lie at an endpoint of the interval [-2,4].
Therefore a maximising n-tuple has the form

  4, \ldots , 4 k times; -2, \ldots , -2 m times; (if necessary) one interior value z,   (4)

with k+m = n or k+m = n-1.  The zero-sum condition forces

  4k - 2m + z = 0   (5)

(the term z is absent if k+m = n).

We now distinguish the residue of n modulo 3.

--------------------
Case A: 3 | n (n = 3q).  
Choose k = q, m = 2q and omit z.  Equation (5) is satisfied
and every entry is an endpoint, so this is feasible.
Then  

  T_j = 16k + 4m = 16q + 8q = 24q = 8n.        (6)

No other choice can yield a larger T_j because replacing any interior z by an
endpoint preserves (5) and maintains or increases T_j, and any different split
(k,m) with only endpoints violates (5).

--------------------
Case B: n \equiv  1 (mod 3) (n = 3q + 1, q \geq  0).  
Here it is impossible to satisfy (5) using only endpoints.
Take k = q fours, m = 2q twos, and let the remaining
single entry be z = 0.  Then (5) holds and  

  T_j = 16q + 4\cdot (2q) + 0^2 = 24q = 8(n - 1).   (7)

Any competing arrangement must still have k fours and m twos
(because pushing to endpoints is never harmful) and hence has the same squared
sum, so (7) is the maximum.

--------------------
Case C: n \equiv  2 (mod 3) (n = 3q + 2, q \geq  0).  
This time choose k = q fours, m = 2q + 1 twos, and the solitary interior
entry z = 2.  Equation (5) becomes 4q - 2(2q+1) + 2 = 0, which is true.
Now  

  T_j = 16q + 4\cdot (2q+1) + 2^2 = 16q + 8q + 4 + 4 = 24q + 8 = 8(n - 1).   (8)

Exactly the same argument as before shows this is optimal.

Step 4.  Collecting the one-dimensional maxima  
Summarising (6)-(8), for every coordinate  

  max T_j =   8n       if 3 | n,  
          8(n-1)  otherwise.                               (9)

Step 5.  Returning to d dimensions  
Since the coordinates are independent and there are d of them,

  T_max = d \cdot  max T_j =   8dn       if 3 | n,  
               8d(n-1) otherwise.                        (10)

Plugging T_max into (1), we finally obtain

  S_max(n,d) = n \cdot  T_max  
      =   8d n^2        if 3 divides n,  
          8d n(n - 1)  if n \equiv  1 or 2 (mod 3).              (11)

Step 6.  Construction realising the bound  
* 3 | n: take exactly n/3 copies of the vector (4,\ldots ,4),  
     and 2n/3 copies of (-2,\ldots ,-2).  
* n \equiv  1 (mod 3): take q copies of (4,\ldots ,4), 2q copies of (-2,\ldots ,-2),
     and one copy of (0,\ldots ,0).  
* n \equiv  2 (mod 3): take q copies of (4,\ldots ,4), 2q+1 copies of (-2,\ldots ,-2),
     and one copy of (2,\ldots ,2).  
In each case the centroid is 0 and equality in (11) is achieved.

Hence the required maximum is exactly the quantity displayed in (11).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The problem now takes place in ℝᵈ and involves Euclidean
   distances of vectors, not just real numbers.

2. Additional global constraint:  The centroid of the entire configuration is
   fixed at the origin, coupling all coordinates and all vectors simultaneously.

3. Multi-layered reduction:  One must first transform the pairwise
   energy (★), then decouple coordinates, then solve a constrained quadratic
   optimisation in one variable, and finally re-assemble the results.

4. Modular arithmetic distinction:  The optimal value depends in a subtle way
   on n (mod 3); recognising and proving these three separate cases is
   essential.

5. Convex-extremal reasoning in combination with combinatorics:
   The proof that “all but at most one coordinate sit at an endpoint’’ employs
   convexity, while deciding which endpoint pattern is best requires careful
   counting arguments.

6. Non-trivial construction:  Exhibiting configurations that hit the bound
   in each congruence class demands simultaneous satisfaction of
   interval bounds, the centroid condition, and maximality of the quadratic
   form.

All these layers go well beyond the original and current kernel variants,
which only involved one-dimensional variables with simple interval bounds and
no global balancing condition.

original_kernel_variant.question:

  S(n,d) =  \Sigma _{1 \leq  i<j \leq  n}  \|x_i - x_j\|_2^2.
